caveats: a: BLS evidence covers all NAICS 335.
a: Firmware and industrial software work may sit in separate establishments or occupations, making boundary measurement difficult.
rho: General manufacturing surveys may not represent smaller controls firms.
rho: High exposure does not imply autonomous release of safety-critical products.
e: The frozen count is margin-bridged, not a verified target list.
e: NAICS boundaries between controls, instruments, electronic components, and integration services can be porous.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
s5: Product-line acquisitions may not be qualifying firm control deals.
q: Economics vary between standard relays, configured drives, motor-control centers, embedded software, and services.
q: Large-platform vendors may have stronger pricing power than the target firms.
d5: Public-company growth includes software and services outside the narrow manufacturing construct.
d5: Sales measures are nominal and affected by acquisitions, price, geography, and market share.
o: Integrated electronics may eliminate discrete products while preserving the control function.
o: A shift to large automation platforms can displace LMM firms without eliminating the need for an accountable supplier.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Products are sold through repeat OEM and distributor channels, configured-to-order purchases, and automation projects, with recurring replacement, expansion, spare, software, engineering, and lifecycle-service demand around installed controls.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, pure distributors or integrators without qualifying manufacturing, and environmental or process-variable instrument makers classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers supplying relays, motor starters and controllers, drives, configured control products, industrial control accessories, and related repeat engineering or lifecycle support to external OEM, industrial, infrastructure, and automation customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 335 occupation mix, not wage-weighted task exposure in 335314. I map physical assembly, wiring, testing, fabrication, and material handling to low exposure while assigning higher exposure to product engineering, firmware and configuration, application support, quoting, documentation, scheduling, procurement, quality analysis, sales support, and administration. The proxy may understate this industry's engineering and software intensity or overstate substitution where accountable safety design remains mandatory.
caveats: BLS evidence covers all NAICS 335.
Firmware and industrial software work may sit in separate establishments or occupations, making boundary measurement difficult.
a.change_evidence: A 335314 occupational wage matrix and task-level time studies across relay, drive, motor-control, and configured-control firms would materially refine exposure.
a.rationale: The industry's high compensation ratio and mix of configured hardware, software, engineering, and application support create more AI-exposed work than many metal-product manufacturers, while assembly and validation remain physical.

d5.bridge: Rockwell Automation is a large global platform vendor rather than a US LMM 335314 firm. Its fiscal-2025 organic sales rose 1%, recurring ARR rose 8%, and fiscal-2026 organic guidance was 2% to 6%; separate semiconductor evidence identifies growing factory-automation and motion-control demand. I translate these nominal, mixed-business signals into moderate five-year constant-price quantity growth. The proxy may overstate software-led growth or understate niche hardware demand from reshoring and labor scarcity.
caveats: Public-company growth includes software and services outside the narrow manufacturing construct.
Sales measures are nominal and affected by acquisitions, price, geography, and market share.
d5.change_evidence: NEMA or Census six-digit constant-dollar shipments and bookings split by relay, starter, drive, motor controller, configured system, and lifecycle support would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Factory automation, robotics, reshoring, electrification, energy efficiency, safety, and replacement of legacy controls support growth. Capital-spending cycles, customer inventory corrections, standardization, embedded controls, and consolidation constrain it.

caveats: The frozen count is margin-bridged, not a verified target list.
NAICS boundaries between controls, instruments, electronic components, and integration services can be porous.
e.change_evidence: A verified review of the 210 dataset-estimated LMM firms covering ownership, manufacturing content, IP, certifications, customer concentration, recurring revenue, and management depth would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The industry supports independent niche relay, motor-control, configured-product, and accessory manufacturers with transferable IP, teams, and customer approvals. Multinational plants, captive units, distributor-integrators, owner-dependent engineering shops, and firms whose value is inseparable from one product expert reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 335314 ($4,045,556,277) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 335314 ($10,965,641,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $323,394 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 335314); class <5: 200 firms, avg revenue/firm $590,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $115,797 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 99 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,999,818 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $392,364 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,382,917 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $663,728 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 168 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,067,845 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,171,511 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,632,786 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,402,553 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 60 firms, avg revenue/firm $118,248,067 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $23,200,271 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 210

caveats: Integrated electronics may eliminate discrete products while preserving the control function.
A shift to large automation platforms can displace LMM firms without eliminating the need for an accountable supplier.
o.change_evidence: OEM product roadmaps and bill-of-material studies showing discrete-to-integrated control migration, software-defined control, direct configuration, and insourcing would materially change operator requirement.
o.rationale: Industrial machines still require physical protection, switching, motor control, sensing interfaces, safety, and accountable hardware and software integration. Some discrete relay and accessory quantity can be absorbed into programmable, networked, or solid-state platforms or customer-configured software.

caveats: Economics vary between standard relays, configured drives, motor-control centers, embedded software, and services.
Large-platform vendors may have stronger pricing power than the target firms.
q.change_evidence: LMM contracts and channel data showing price-reset frequency, distributor margins, rebates, OEM productivity clauses, support pricing, renewal retention, and post-improvement margins would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Proprietary products, qualification, installed-base compatibility, engineering support, and switching costs preserve some benefit. OEM negotiations, distributor discounts, competitive product comparisons, annual price resets, and software competition pass part of gains to customers over five years.

caveats: General manufacturing surveys may not represent smaller controls firms.
High exposure does not imply autonomous release of safety-critical products.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at LMM control manufacturers showing engineering hours, defect escapes, test coverage, support resolution, configuration errors, release controls, and customer acceptance would materially move implementation.
rho.rationale: Code assistance, configuration, document search, application engineering, quoting, test generation, support triage, scheduling, and quality analytics are implementable within five years. Functional safety, cybersecurity, validation, legacy compatibility, product liability, and customer qualification require human controls.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among US employer-business owners across industries, with 22% reporting such plans. I use that as the base anchor, with a small upward allowance for strategic interest in automation technology and a downward allowance for failed or nonqualifying transfers. The proxy may overstate completed deals or understate unsolicited strategic acquisitions.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
Product-line acquisitions may not be qualifying firm control deals.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of completed private 335314 transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, industrial-automation advisers, and trade publications, divided by a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Succession and strategic demand create regular transfer potential, but IP diligence, customer qualification, founder technical dependence, cybersecurity exposure, and integration risk can prevent closing.

principal_driver: Engineering-heavy workflows and rising automation demand create both productivity and durable-demand opportunities.
principal_weakness: Platform consolidation and software-defined controls can erode discrete products and LMM bargaining power.
